segments.text: Imagine that you're driving a car in the
middle of the road and your car breaks
down. What do you do? You get out of the
car. You check the tires. Okay, so the
tires are fine. Let's say you're going
to go to the next step. Maybe look at
the hood of the car. Look at the
battery. And that's where you find the
problem. The battery is the problem. So,
you get to work on the battery. Now,
imagine for a second if when your car
broke down, you decided to just take the
car and throw the entire car away. How
silly would that be? But sometimes
that's exactly what we do when we're
overthinking. We start as soon as we
start to feel a negative emotion, we
just jump. We directly jump to the worst
possible scenario in your head and we
catastrophize the whole thing. But why
do we overthink at all? What is the need
or the what is the purpose of
overthinking? The first reason why we
overthink is because it gives us a false
sense of control. Because when we have
gaps in our information, we tend to try
and look at something from 50 different
angles and assume that uh looking at it
from so many different angles is going
to give me my answers.
